name: Almendra Reservoir and Fermoselle Loop
name_original: Embalse de Almendra y Fermoselle — circular por el Parque Natural de Arribes del Duero
description: Cycle a moderate 32-mile route around Embalse de Almendra and Fermoselle, enjoying dramatic canyons and the vast reservoir in Arribes del Du
region_intro: Road cycling around Almendra, Portugal, offers diverse landscapes ranging from well-paved surfaces to challenging routes with significant elevation gain. The region is characterized by its varied terrain, including segments that venture into the Côa Valley with steep inclines. Cyclists can experience routes along the scenic Douro River, which provides expansive views and is part of a protected zone. The area presents a mix of easy, moderate, and hard road cycling routes, catering to different skill and fitness levels. Best road cycling routes around Almendra The most popular road cycling route is Almendra Reservoir and Fermoselle Loop, a 32.0 miles (51.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 31 minutes to complete. This moderate route features rolling terrain around the reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Embalse de Almendra – return through the villages of Sayago, a moderate 39.1 miles (62.9 km) path. This route offers views of the reservoir and passes through traditional villages. Local road cyclists also love the Bemposta Dam and Fermoselle Loop, a 32.3 miles (51.9 km) trail leading through the area surrounding the Bemposta Dam, often completed in about 2 hours 34 minutes. Road cycling around Almendra is defined by the Douro River, the challenging Côa Valley, and varied terrain with both paved and unpaved sections. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Almendra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 24 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Almendra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Almendra, and what are their difficulty levels?
A: Almendra offers a selection of 7 road cycling routes. The majority, 5 routes, are classified as moderate, while 2 routes are considered difficult, providing options for cyclists with varying fitness levels.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes in Almendra for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, Almendra features challenging road cycling routes, particularly those venturing into the Côa Valley. These routes often include steep inclines and can even have unpaved segments, requiring good fitness. An example of a more demanding route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20141280" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Fermoselle – Almendra Reservoir loop from Camping Los Arribes&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 67 km with significant elevation gain.

Q: What kind of scenery can I expect on road cycling routes in Almendra?
A: Road cycling in Almendra offers diverse and scenic landscapes. You can expect routes along the majestic Douro River, providing expansive views, and challenging terrain through the dramatic Côa Valley. The region features a mix of well-paved roads and some unpaved sections, passing through traditional villages and natural protected zones.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in Almendra?
A: While the region's terrain can be varied, with many routes featuring moderate to difficult sections and significant elevation, there are no specifically designated 'easy' routes. Cyclists with families should assess their fitness and experience carefully. Routes with rolling terrain around the reservoir, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1026436189" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almendra Reservoir and Fermoselle Loop&lt;/a&gt;, might be more manageable for those with some cycling experience.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes around Almendra?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Almendra are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1026436189" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almendra Reservoir and Fermoselle Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1023175510" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bemposta Dam and Fermoselle Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What interesting attractions or landmarks can I visit while road cycling near Almendra?
A: The region around Almendra offers several points of interest. You can visit the Côa Museum, which showcases early human rock drawings, located where the Douro and Côa rivers meet. The impressive Ponte Almirante Sarmento Rodrigues, known for its engineering, is also a notable landmark. Along the routes, you might encounter natural monuments and viewpoints such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4791099" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Staircase Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4899808" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Picão da Bouça D'Aires Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Almendra?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, from well-paved surfaces to challenging climbs, and the stunning views, particularly along the Douro River. The mix of moderate and difficult routes also caters to a wide range of abilities.

Q: Are there routes that offer views of the Almendra Reservoir?
A: Absolutely. Several routes provide excellent views of the Almendra Reservoir. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025474527" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Almendra – return through the villages of Sayago&lt;/a&gt; route, for instance, offers expansive views of the reservoir and passes through traditional villages in the Sayago region.

Q: What is the typical duration for road cycling routes in Almendra?
A: The duration of road cycling routes in Almendra varies depending on the distance and difficulty. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1026436189" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almendra Reservoir and Fermoselle Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 51.5 km route, typically takes around 2 hours and 31 minutes to complete. Longer and more challenging routes will naturally require more time.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through traditional villages?
A: Yes, some road cycling routes in Almendra offer the opportunity to pass through charming traditional villages. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025474527" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Almendra – return through the villages of Sayago&lt;/a&gt; route is a great example, allowing you to experience the local culture and architecture as you cycle.

Q: What are the road conditions like for cycling in Almendra?
A: The road conditions in Almendra are varied. Many routes feature mostly well-paved surfaces suitable for road cycling. However, especially when venturing into areas like the Côa Valley, you might encounter segments that are unpaved or have rougher surfaces, which can add to the challenge and require appropriate bike choice and fitness.
tips: From the road that comes from the town of Trabanca and when you arrive at Fermoselle, you can see the silhouette of the town that is on top of a small hill with panoramic views of the area.
The road that goes from Fermoselle to Pinilla de Fermoselle is a set of small mountains that give a view of what are the last Spanish lands before crossing the Duero River and reaching Portugal.
Spectacular viewpoint to the Almendra dam, both at the top and at the bottom. Depending on when the reservoir is visited, it is more or less full of water.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and challenges can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as moderate for racebikes, covering approximately 51.5 kilometers with around 780 meters of elevation gain. While it features mostly well-paved surfaces, be prepared for significant climbs, often following descents into the river gorges. These ascents can include 'impressive ramps,' and humidity might increase the difficulty in some sections.

faqs.question: What are the main sights and viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers spectacular views of the dramatic landscapes of the Arribes del Duero Natural Park. You'll see the colossal Almendra Dam, one of Spain's highest, and the vast Almendra Reservoir, often called the 'Sea of Castilla.' Key viewpoints include View of Fermoselle and Altos de la Jarrina, providing expansive panoramas of the canyons.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Arribes del Duero region can experience hot summers and cold winters. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling, with less extreme weather. These seasons also provide beautiful natural scenery as the flora changes.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, Fermoselle is a common starting point for routes in this area. You can typically find parking within or near the town to begin your ride. Always check local signage for parking regulations.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a road cycling route, it's generally not suitable for dogs to accompany cyclists directly on the road due to safety concerns. If you plan to visit the Parque Natural de Arribes del Duero with a dog, ensure they are kept on a leash in protected areas and respect local regulations regarding pets in natural parks.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Parque Natural de Arribes del Duero?
faqs.answer: No, there are typically no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Parque Natural de Arribes del Duero. However, always respect the natural environment and any specific regulations posted within the park, as it is a protected area and a UNESCO cross-border biosphere reserve.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the Ruta del Duero and also touches upon parts of [CIMA ZM01] Alto de Fermoselle, Etapa 32: Fermoselle – Trabanca, Etapa 30: Fornillos de Fermoselle – Pinilla de Fermoselle, and Etapa 31: Pinilla de Fermoselle – Fermoselle.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular route is approximately 51.5 kilometers long and typically takes around 2 hours and 30 minutes to complete for a racebike cyclist, depending on your pace and stops for enjoying the views.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families or beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty, significant elevation changes (around 780 meters), and 'impressive ramps,' this route is best suited for cyclists with good fitness and some experience in road cycling. It might be challenging for families with young children or absolute beginners.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Fermoselle, a town along the route, is a good place to find amenities. You can expect to find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options there, making it a convenient stop for refreshments or an overnight stay.

faqs.question: What makes this particular cycling route unique?
faqs.answer: This route offers a unique combination of natural beauty and engineering marvel. You'll cycle through the dramatic canyons of the Parque Natural de Arribes del Duero, a protected area with unique geomorphology and biodiversity, while also passing by the impressive Almendra Dam, a significant hydroelectric feat. The sheer scale of the canyons, reaching up to 400 meters in vertical drop, provides a truly memorable backdrop.
